topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in large language models between 2020 and 2024. One of the key areas of research has been the application of large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry (Bran et al., 2024) and medicine (Singhal et al., 2025). For example, large language models have been used to generate chemical reactions and predict chemical properties (Bran et al., 2024). In the medical domain, large language models have been used for a range of tasks, including medical question answering (Singhal et al., 2025) and clinical text summarization (Van Veen et al., 2024).

The use of retrieval-augmented generation techniques has also been explored in large language models, with studies showing that this approach can improve the accuracy and diversity of generated text (Gao et al., 2023). Additionally, research has been conducted on the explainability of large language models, with studies highlighting the importance of transparency and interpretability in model outputs (Zhao et al., 2024). Other studies have focused on the application of large language models to specific tasks, such as generating trustworthy health supplement content (Holter et al., 2018).

There are also studies that have explored the use of large language models in autonomous agents (Wang et al., 2024). These studies have shown that large language models can be effective in improving the accuracy and efficiency of autonomous agents. However, there are also challenges associated with the use of large language models in these areas, such as the need for high-quality training data and the potential for bias in model outputs (Zhao et al., 2024).

In terms of agreements and contradictions, there is a general consensus in the literature that large language models have the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of a range of tasks, including medical question answering and clinical text summarization (Singhal et al., 2025; Van Veen et al., 2024). However, there are also disagreements about the best approach to achieving this, with some studies advocating for the use of retrieval-augmented generation techniques (Gao et al., 2023) and others highlighting the importance of explainability and transparency in model outputs (Zhao et al., 2024).

Research gaps in this area include the need for further studies on the application of large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry and medicine (Bran et al., 2024; Singhal et al., 2025). Additionally, there is a need for further research on the explainability and transparency of large language models, particularly in high-stakes applications such as medicine (Zhao et al., 2024). Furthermore, studies have also explored the role of AI in hospitals and clinics, highlighting the potential of large language models to transform healthcare in the 21st century (Maleki Varnosfaderani et al., 2024).

References:
Bran, A. M., Cox, S., & Schilter, O. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
